G02Q10: Using the toolset is intuitive, and the using comments to ask AI agents allows for the prompt to be targeted towards a particular section.
G02Q10Copy:
Many of the features are not something I would need: I never need to translate text I have written, unless for very specific purposes where I would use a targeted translation tool for a whole body of text. The summary shortcut is not useful, since I never need to summarise my own text, and when proof reading a collaborators text, I want to proof read their actual text and not a summary. A summary might be useful if the goal is to make a paragraph more concise, but this is not consistently achieved by genAI.

A minor complaint about comments: the width of the comment box is by default quite small, but the AI answers are usually extremely verbose and long, making it difficult to read. Furthermore, genAI typically uses a lot of formatting (famously: lists, emdashes, boldfaced keywords, etc.), but these would not show up in the comments, making a lot of the genAI text unreadable until put into the document.
G02Q07: It was easy to communicate with an AI agent in the comments.
G02Q07Copy: I found the agents to be faily indistinguishable. I believe this could be partially my fault by choosing the wrong keywords. The ones I used gave generic AI answers, where the main difference was a repetition of what that particular agent's instructions are (sometimes without following those instructions).
G02Q08: The tasks did what I expected. When scanning a full text, the task would integrate well with the comment feature and highlight text that could be changed. This worked very well.
G02Q08Copy:
There was no good way to "undo" the results of a task once integrated with the text, since the agent is a separate author, rather than myself (where I could just use "undo"). This was very frustrating, and I often ended up having to manually remove all the agent edits and redo the task (and sometimes simply do the task myself). 

The worst aspect is the lack of accountability. I believe it is important that AI agents and their tasks are connected to a real author. In a collaborative setting, if a co-author uses a task, I would still consider this to be their edits (and their responsibility to proof read!). If something is wrong (e.g. AI hallucination), we need to be able to blame the author rather than the AI agent (especially if we collaborate on scientific text). As it is currently, the lack of accountability means I would not use this tool in scientific writing.
G02Q09: The prototype in general felt like using a polished modern collaborative writing tool such as Google docs. The new features were integrated seemlessly without bugs.
G02Q09Copy:
First, I want to highlight that my position is slightly biased, as I never use AI for collaborative writing. In my particular field of study, using AI to write text works poorly and is generally not accepted. On a personal level, I tend to regard text produced by AI to be overly verbose and lack precision. 

For this study, we decided to use the tool for a more personal and creative use. My goal was to see if AI could help me feel more creative, and produce a usable end result in a shorter amount of time. While the AI agents were useful to get started, I got frustrated with the resulting text, as I found it fell into all the pitfalls of typical genAI (verbose, generic, without heart and often wrong). 

I am currently inclined to start over and write the text without AI for a better result. 

I also found the lack of accountability (once integrated, there was no good way to see which text was generated, and especially by which author) concerning. At times, I felt like I was babysitting AI agents rather than make actual progress. I would have prefered to use a chatbot style AI, and manually copy paste what I need while making edits, to feel more in control of the text I write (if I use AI to write my text, I am still responsible for guaranteeing that what I am inserting into the document is true, and well-written).

G02Q10:
Got it — here are the reformulated bullet points in plain ASCII with `*`:

* The toolset (and essentially the entire interface) was presented in a clear and organized way.
* Accessing the tools required little effort.
* The connection between text sections and comments was clearly visible, just as expected.
* The tooling overall felt very familiar and was therefore intuitive.
G02Q10Copy:
* The answers of the AIs are often rather long and are hardly readable by just screening the comments. 
* Accepting suggestions was sometimes a bit cumbersome when generating several suggestions at once. A parser that could extract the usable texts from the suggestions individually might be helpful.
* I would expect that when I reply to an AI’s answer in the comments, I can then receive new results. However, this does only happen if I mention @<target AI> in my answer. Otherwise, the comment will be ignored, which would still be okay (we have an ambiguity here). However, if I edit the comment so that the comment mentions the target AI, the comment is not processed by the AI.
* I could not immediately see how to deactivate the AI Agent Joker. I did not want to delete it directly. Activating such a Joker is rather discouraging, since it floods everything with comments.
G02Q07:
* I was able to move very quickly from initial thoughts to a fully formulated text.
* It felt good to create different agents for different specialized writing tasks.
* Creating the agents was very easy.
* I found it easy to break down my own writing task into subtasks that were suitable for individual agents (such as content generator, proofreader, etc.).
* The response times did not hinder my workflow, which was a pleasant surprise.
G02Q07Copy:
* The agent for improving formulations turned out to be surprisingly poor.
* The agents' CVs have been a small hurdle as they require to specify role, expertise and skills and I struggled a bit to get an idea what kind of information is expected in the corresponding fields.
* It would be nice to have a testing environment for agents without the need to start a task or mentioning them in a comment. Especially regarding your "Likert question" from above ("The information I entered on the AI agent profile page influenced its answer") cannot be answered as it is hard to say what would happen without custom agents. The testing stage could provide a dummy task auto-generated based on the CV to test the effectiveness of the agent's profile.
* Still I find AI agents most useful if you already know what you want to write. So I consider them rather as an accelerator for writing than a knowledge source or "thinking companion".
G02Q08:
* It was extremely easy to create and run tasks.
* They have been real accelerators in the writing tasks. 
* I really like the idea of separating the description of the "persona" of the agent from its concrete tasks. I can imagine that defining a "task library" could really be of much value in writing tasks.
G02Q08Copy:
* The shortcuts for tasks were too inconspicuous. I could not associate the bullet-point list icons with their intended function.
* The autonomous task executions rather increased the effort due to too many comments that are generated with growing length of the text. So I would like to have a trigger like "finished paragraph" and the agent then only checks this paragraph.
G02Q09:
* It seemed familiar enough for me to find my way around immediately, but then, at second glance, it offered completely new features that were also very easy to access.
* Although it is a prototype, this was rarely noticeable in the tool.
G02Q09Copy:
* At one point, all of my comments were deleted and replaced by the comments of the already deleted Joker task.
* First we were not sure if the agents are document- oder user-specific. Maybe it is communicated somewhere but if so, we've overlooked this detail. Should be made more explicit. 
* Sometimes it seemed to have lost my authentication (?). Exceptions were thrown but no user feedback was available. So I reloaded the page to continue working.

G02Q10: using AI capabilities without switching context appears very useful to me (when output and formats fit expectations). The interaction modes were familiar from other text editor tools.
G02Q10Copy:
Extending bullet points did not give me useful results. 

After I created a comment with a task for an agent, I don’t get immediate feedback what is next. I wondered if I had to do something to start the task or if that is already happening.
G02Q07: I really like the idea to create agents for different aspects of my writing tasks like research, structuring, wording, different areas of expertise etc.
G02Q07Copy: In this writing task I felt that creating an agent for a task was not yet making me more efficient. My gut feeling was that it would probably accelerate my work across similar documents when I would be able to reuse agents (that stand the test of time / multiple tasks) - like a team of writing assistants.
G02Q08: The idea makes sense (but I used the comment feature for LLM tasks instead).
G02Q08Copy: I was more inclined to create tasks for agents with the comments @ feature, as this is more similar to the ways of working I'm used to, and it was easier to think of specific tasks for agents anchored in the document for me.
G02Q09: I really like the interface to organize agents to reuse them in writing tasks.
G02Q09Copy:
In this task the set-up did not make me more efficient yet. That is probably also because I first had to get used to the feature set and I wasn't always happy with the agents output so I did a lot of deleting and editing here. 
I also found myself using LLMs in parallel for the same writing task in other browser windows - for example because I wanted it to analyze documents like PDFs or because I needed to LLM to organize information in an excel sheet format. For those tasks the agent set-up wasn't versatile enough (yet) or I didn't know how to use it for that. 

When defining agents I wasn't sure about the difference between expertise and skill set.

G02Q08:
Allowed some mundane tasks (like checking spelling / grammar or form) to be performed automatically.
I also really like the option the integrate the custom tasks into the tool bar.
G02Q08Copy: Automatically performed tasks often gave too many comments on the text, felt overwhelming.
G02Q09:
The prototype was really easy to use.
I really like the idea of integrating AI authors into the cooperative writing environment.
The tasks were a great option for outsourcing mundane tasks to AI agents.
G02Q09Copy:
The underlying AI models often didn't seem powerful enough for the tasks at hand, therefore the feedback often times wasn't up to the expectations.
The prototype would really benefit from a direct chat feature, instead of going the extra way of defining specific tasks or needing to reference specific text passages.
Slight inaccuracies in referencing the text by the AI agents (passages are often incomplete or contain unrelated text)
